items.title: Acute vs chronic anterior uveitis
items.contentHtml:
Acute vs chronic anterior uveitis

  • Acute anterior uveitis has sudden onset, duration <3 months, pain, photophobia, perilimbic hyperemia and Tyndall positive.
  • Chronic anterior uveitis persists >3 months, often asymptomatic, with low-grade anterior chamber cells and greater risk of complications such as posterior synechiae.
  • The acute one is associated with HLA-B27 and spondyloarthropathies; chronic to juvenile idiopathic arthritis, sarcoidosis or Fuchs syndrome.

items.title: Granulomatous vs non-granulomatous classification
items.contentHtml:
Granulomatous vs non-granulomatous classification

  • Granulomatous anterior uveitis: large keratic precipitates, in sheep fat (mutton-fat KP), Koeppe and Busacca nodules, and posterior synechiae frequent.
  • Causes: sarcoidosis, syphilis, tuberculosis, herpes zoster and Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease.
  • Non-granulomatous anterior uveitis: fine precipitates, cells and flare without nodules; associated with HLA-B27 (ankylosing spondylitis, Reiter), juvenile idiopathic arthritis and herpes simplex.

items.foco: Fuchs syndrome as chronic anterior uveitis
items.title: Fuchs syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Fuchs syndrome as chronic anterior uveitis

  • Fuchs syndrome (Fuchs heterochromia): unilateral, asymptomatic chronic anterior uveitis, with diffuse stellate keratic precipitates, diffuse atrophy of the iris and posterior subcapsular cataract.
  • No synchiae posteriornor pain; Mild Tyndall but persistent cells.
  • Treatment with topical corticosteroids is ineffective; vitrectomy may be indicated if there are vitreous opacities.
  • Main complication: secondary glaucoma due to trabeculitis.

items.foco: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis and anterior uveitis
items.title: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis
items.contentHtml:
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis and anterior uveitis

  • Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) (oligoarticular, ANA+) is the most common cause of chronic anterior uveitis in children.
  • Asymptomatic, bilateral, non-granulomatous uveitis, with Tyndall and cells, but without pain or photophobia until late stages.
  • Ophthalmological screening is mandatory: every 3-4 months in high-risk patients (ANA+, oligoarticular, <7 years).
  • First line: topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1%) and cycloplegics (cyclopentolate, atropine).
  • Refractory: methotrexate, then anti-TNF (adalimumab).

items.title: Ankylosing spondylitis and HLA-B27
items.contentHtml:
Ankylosing spondylitis and HLA-B27

  • Ankylosing spondylitis is associated with HLA-B27 in >90% of cases; Anterior uveitis is acute, unilateral, recurrent, non-granulomatous.
  • Typical presentation: ocular pain, photophobia, lacrimation, Tyndall and cells in the anterior chamber, posterior synechiae if not dilated.
  • First line: topical corticosteroids (prednisolone 1% every hour) and cycloplegics (cyclopentolate 1% or atropine 1%) to break synechiae.
  • Severe cases: systemic corticosteroids or immunomodulators (anti-TNF).

items.title: Reiter syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Reiter's syndrome and anterior uveitis

  • Reiter's syndrome (reactive arthritis) is associated with HLA-B27 and infectious triggers: Chlamydia trachomatis or enterobacteria (Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia).
  • Classic triad: urethritis, arthritis and conjunctivitis; Anterior uveitis is acute, unilateral, non-granulomatous.
  • Treatment: topical corticosteroids and cycloplegic; if recurrent, consider systemic NSAIDs or sulfasalazine.
  • Differentiate from ankylosing spondylitis: in Reiter there is a history of urogenital or intestinal infection.

items.title: Syphilis and anterior uveitis
items.contentHtml:
Syphilis as an infectious cause

  • Syphilis (Treponema pallidum) can cause granulomatous anterior uveitis in secondary and tertiary stages.
  • Characteristics: mutton-fat keratic precipitates, Koeppe nodules, posterior synechiae and sometimes interstitial keratitis.
  • Diagnosis: serology (VDRL/RPR confirmed with FTA-ABS or MHA-TP).
  • Treatment: aqueous penicillin G 18-24 million IU/day IV for 14 days; Avoid topical corticosteroids until antibiotics are started to avoid masking.
  • All patients with granulomatous uveitis must have serology for syphilis.

items.foco: Herpes simplex and zoster as viral causes
items.title: Herpes simplex and zoster
items.contentHtml:
Herpes simplex and zoster as viral causes

  • Herpes simplex (HSV) and herpes zoster (VZV) cause unilateral anterior uveitis, sometimes with associated keratitis and sectoral atrophy of the iris.
  • HSV: non-granulomatous or granulomatous uveitis; VZV: most frequently granulomatous with mutton-fat precipitates.
  • Diagnosis: clinical; PCR of aqueous humor in atypical cases.
  • Treatment: topical acyclovir or oral valacyclovir (1 g every 8 hours), topical corticosteroids (prednisolone 1%) and cycloplegics.
  • Do not use corticosteroids alone without antiviral due to risk of exacerbation.

items.title: Sarcoidosis and anterior uveitis
items.contentHtml:
Sarcoidosis and granulomatous anterior uveitis

  • Sarcoidosis is a classic cause of bilateral granulomatous anterior uveitis, with mutton-fat precipitates, Koeppe and Busacca nodules, and posterior synechiae.
  • Associated findings: Choroidal granulomas, vitritis, retinal periphlebitis (in drops of candle wax). if unresponsive, systemic corticosteroids or methotrexate.

items.foco: Posterior synechiae as a key complication
items.title: Posterior synechiae
items.contentHtml:
Posterior synechiae as a key complication

  • Posterior synechiae are adhesions between the iris and the anterior capsule of the lens, secondary to inflammation.
  • Complication: pupillary block, secondary glaucoma due to angle closure and cataract.
  • Prevention: use of cycloplegics (cyclopentolate 1% or atropine 1%) to keep the pupil dilated and break incipient synechiae.
  • If they are already formed, an attempt can be made to break them with forced dilation (phenylephrine + cycloplegic) or laser iridotomy.

items.foco: Tyndall and cells in the anterior chamber
items.title: Tyndall and cells in the anterior chamber
items.contentHtml:
Tyndall and cells in the anterior chamber

  • Tyndall (flare) is the opacity of the aqueous humor due to proteins (broken blood-aqueous barrier); It is graded from 0 to 4+.
  • Cells in the anterior chamber are inflammatory leukocytes; They are counted in a 1 mm x 1 mm field in a slit lamp: 0= <1 cell, 1+= 1-5, 2+= 6-15, 3+= 16-25, 4+= >25.
  • Tyndall without cells suggests previous inflammation or chronic uveitis; Cells without Tyndall indicate active inflammation.
  • Grading according to Standardization of Uveitis Nomenclature (SUN) is mandatory for reporting.

items.foco: Topical treatment with corticosteroids and cycloplegics
items.title: Topical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Topical treatment with corticosteroids and cycloplegics

  • First line in anterior uveitis: topical corticosteroids such as prednisolone acetate 1% (more powerful) or dexamethasone 0.1%.
  • Initial frequency: every hour while awake, then reduce according to response; do not discontinue abruptly.
  • Cycloplegics: cyclopentolate 1% (short action) or atropine 1% (long action, 2-3 times/day) to prevent and break posterior synechiae and relieve pain due to spasm of the ciliary muscle.
  • Do not use topical corticosteroids if there is suspicion of corneal infection due to herpes without antiviral coverage.
